Provinces of Italy (grey borders), within Regions (solid borders)

The **provinces of Italy** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *province d'Italia*) are the second-level [administrative divisions](/wiki/Administrative_division "Administrative division") of the [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), on an intermediate level between a municipality (*[comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune")*) and a [region](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") (*regione*). Since 2015, provinces have been classified as "institutional bodies of second level".[[1]](#cite_note-1)

There are currently 110 institutional bodies of second level in Italy, including 82 ordinary provinces, 2 autonomous provinces, 4 regional decentralization entities, 6 free municipal consortia, and 15 [metropolitan cities](/wiki/Metropolitan_cities_of_Italy "Metropolitan cities of Italy"), as well as the [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley") region (which also exercises the powers of a province).

Italian provinces (with the exception of the current Sardinian provinces) correspond to the [NUTS 3 regions](/wiki/NUTS_statistical_regions_of_Italy "NUTS statistical regions of Italy").[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Overview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Overview")]

A province of the [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italy "Italy") is composed of many municipalities (*comune*). Usually several provinces together form a region; the region of [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley") is the sole exception—it is not subdivided into provinces, and provincial functions are exercised by the region.

The three main functions devolved to provinces are:

* Local planning and zoning
* Provision of [local police](/wiki/Municipal_police_(Italy) "Municipal police (Italy)") and [fire services](/wiki/Vigili_del_Fuoco "Vigili del Fuoco")
* Transportation regulation (car registration, maintenance of local roads, etc.)

The number of provinces in Italy has been steadily growing in recent years, as many new ones are carved out of older ones. Usually, the province's name is the same as that of its capital city.

According to the 2014 reform, each province is headed by a [President](/wiki/President_of_the_province "President of the province") (or Commissioner) assisted by a legislative body, the [Provincial Council](/wiki/Provincial_Council_(Italy) "Provincial Council (Italy)"), and an executive body, the Provincial Executive. President (Commissioner) and members of Council are elected together by mayors and city councilors of each municipality of the province. The Executive is chaired by the President (Commissioner) who appoint others members, called *[assessori](/wiki/Assessor_(Italy) "Assessor (Italy)")*. Since 2015, the President (Commissioner) and other members of the council will not receive a salary.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

In each province, there is also a Prefect (*[prefetto](/wiki/Prefetto "Prefetto")*), a representative of the central government who heads an agency called *prefettura-ufficio territoriale del governo*. The Questor (*questore*) is the head of State Police (*[Polizia di Stato](/wiki/Polizia_di_Stato "Polizia di Stato")*) in the province and his office is called *questura*. There is also a provincial police force depending from local government, called [provincial police](/wiki/Provincial_police "Provincial police") (*polizia provinciale*).

The [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley") region is not divided into provinces due to its size, but straight to the *comune* level.

[South Tyrol](/wiki/Alto_Adige/S%C3%BCdtirol "Alto Adige/Südtirol") and [Trentino](/wiki/Trentino "Trentino") are autonomous provinces, unlike all other provinces they have the same legislative powers as regions and are not subordinated to [Trentino-Alto Adige/Südtirol](/wiki/Trentino-Alto_Adige/S%C3%BCdtirol "Trentino-Alto Adige/Südtirol"), the region they are part of.

## Type

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Type")]

Based on the most recent legislation, contained in the law of 7 April 2014 n. 56,[[4]](#cite_note-4) the council and the presidents of the provinces of the regions with ordinary statute are elected by restricted suffrage by the mayors and councilors of the province's municipalities, while in the [metropolitan cities](/wiki/Metropolitan_cities_of_Italy "Metropolitan cities of Italy"), the equivalent of the president of the province is the (elective) mayor of the capital, called "metropolitan mayor".

There are other types of entities similar to the provinces in the regions with special statutes: the free municipal consortia in [Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily") and the [Sardinian](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia") provinces are governed by extraordinary commissioners appointed by the respective regional administrations, the autonomous provinces of [Trentino-Alto Adige](/wiki/Trentino-Alto_Adige "Trentino-Alto Adige") each elect its own president, and finally, in [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley"), the functions of the province are carried out by the regional administration (whose president is elected by the regional council).

Italian provinces by population density

* **[Sardinia](/wiki/Sardinia "Sardinia")** — following the outcome of the regional referendums of 2012 it was decreed that such institutions should be reformed or abolished by March 2013 (thus remaining in office until 28 February 2013).[[7]](#cite_note-7) In January 2014 the Sardinian Regional Administrative Court declared "unconstitutional" the abolition of the Sardinian provinces, which occurred in 2013.[[8]](#cite_note-8) In 2016, Sardinian provinces were reformed by Sardinia regional executive: Cagliari became a [metropolitan city](/wiki/Metropolitan_City_of_Cagliari "Metropolitan City of Cagliari"); the provinces [Olbia-Tempio](/wiki/Province_of_Olbia-Tempio "Province of Olbia-Tempio"), [Ogliastra](/wiki/Province_of_Ogliastra "Province of Ogliastra"), [Medio Campidano](/wiki/Province_of_Medio_Campidano "Province of Medio Campidano"), and [Carbonia-Iglesias](/wiki/Province_of_Carbonia-Iglesias "Province of Carbonia-Iglesias") were abolished.[[9]](#cite_note-9) In 2017, Regional council of Sardinia approved the institution of a new province, [South Sardinia](/wiki/Province_of_South_Sardinia "Province of South Sardinia"). It was formed by the municipalities of province of Cagliari that did not join to metropolitan city of Cagliari, and those which formed the provinces of Medio Campidano and Carbonia Iglesias.[[10]](#cite_note-10) South Sardinia was disestablished on 1 June 2025 and the other reinstated, some with different names, and Sassari made a metropolitan city.[[11]](#cite_note-1giugno2025-11)
* **[Sicily](/wiki/Sicily "Sicily")** — provinces were replaced by six free municipal consortia in 2013 and three [metropolitan cities](/wiki/Metropolitan_cities_of_Italy "Metropolitan cities of Italy") in 2015.[[12]](#cite_note-12)
* **[Friuli-Venezia Giulia](/wiki/Friuli-Venezia_Giulia "Friuli-Venezia Giulia")** — in 2016, the regional council of Friuli-Venezia Giulia approved a law which abolished the four provinces that formed the region, and replaced them by 18 territorial unions of municipalities.[[13]](#cite_note-13) In 2019, the regional council of Friuli-Venezia Giulia rebranded the four provinces as the four regional decentralization entities, with their own competences, powers, and capital.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
* **[Metropolitan cities](/wiki/Metropolitan_cities_of_Italy "Metropolitan cities of Italy")** — in 2015, 14 metropolitan cities replaced the provinces of [Bari](/wiki/Province_of_Bari "Province of Bari"), [Bologna](/wiki/Province_of_Bologna "Province of Bologna"), [Cagliari](/wiki/Province_of_Cagliari "Province of Cagliari"), [Catania](/wiki/Province_of_Catania "Province of Catania"), [Florence](/wiki/Province_of_Florence "Province of Florence"), [Genoa](/wiki/Province_of_Genoa "Province of Genoa"), [Messina](/wiki/Province_of_Messina "Province of Messina"), [Milan](/wiki/Province_of_Milan "Province of Milan"), [Naples](/wiki/Province_of_Naples "Province of Naples"), [Palermo](/wiki/Province_of_Palermo "Province of Palermo"), [Reggio Calabria](/wiki/Province_of_Reggio_Calabria "Province of Reggio Calabria"), [Rome](/wiki/Province_of_Rome "Province of Rome"), [Turin](/wiki/Province_of_Turin "Province of Turin"), and [Venice](/wiki/Province_of_Venice "Province of Venice").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: History")]

### National unification

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: National unification")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d4/Council_room_of_the_Province_of_Chieti.jpg/250px-Council_room_of_the_Province_of_Chieti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Council_room_of_the_Province_of_Chieti.jpg)

Council room of the [Province of Chieti](/wiki/Province_of_Chieti "Province of Chieti") built in the first half of the 20th century in [Neoclassical style](/wiki/Neoclassical_style "Neoclassical style")

In 1861, at the [birth of the Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Birth_of_the_Kingdom_of_Italy "Birth of the Kingdom of Italy"), there were 59 provinces. However, at that time the national territory was smaller than the current one: [regions](/wiki/Regions_of_Italy "Regions of Italy") of [Veneto](/wiki/Veneto "Veneto"), [Friuli-Venezia Giulia](/wiki/Friuli-Venezia_Giulia "Friuli-Venezia Giulia"), [Trentino-Alto Adige/Südtirol](/wiki/Trentino-Alto_Adige/S%C3%BCdtirol "Trentino-Alto Adige/Südtirol"), and [Lazio](/wiki/Lazio "Lazio") were not included in the kingdom.

In 1866, following the [Third Independence War](/wiki/Third_Independence_War "Third Independence War"), territories of Veneto, [Friuli](/wiki/Friuli "Friuli") and [Mantua](/wiki/Mantua "Mantua") were annexed. There were therefore nine more provinces: [Belluno](/wiki/Province_of_Belluno "Province of Belluno"), [Mantua](/wiki/Province_of_Mantua "Province of Mantua"), [Padua](/wiki/Province_of_Padua "Province of Padua"), [Rovigo](/wiki/Province_of_Rovigo "Province of Rovigo"), [Treviso](/wiki/Province_of_Treviso "Province of Treviso"), [Venice](/wiki/Province_of_Venezia "Province of Venezia"), [Verona](/wiki/Province_of_Verona "Province of Verona"), [Vicenza](/wiki/Province_of_Vicenza "Province of Vicenza"), and [Udine](/wiki/Province_of_Udine "Province of Udine"), all previously part of the [Austrian Empire](/wiki/Austrian_Empire "Austrian Empire"). Eventually, in 1870, following the union of [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") and [its province](/wiki/Province_of_Rome "Province of Rome") from the [Papal States](/wiki/Papal_States "Papal States"), the provinces rose in number to 69.

After the [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), new territories were annexed to Italy. The [province of Trento](/wiki/Province_of_Trento "Province of Trento") was created in 1923. Provinces of [La Spezia](/wiki/Province_of_La_Spezia "Province of La Spezia") and [Trieste](/wiki/Province_of_Trieste "Province of Trieste") in 1923, while [Ionio](/wiki/Province_of_Taranto "Province of Taranto") in 1924.
In 1924 the new provinces of [Fiume](/wiki/Province_of_Fiume "Province of Fiume"), [Pola](/wiki/Province_of_Pola "Province of Pola"), and [Zara](/wiki/Province_of_Zara "Province of Zara") were created, increasing the total number of provinces in Italy to 76.

### Interwar period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Interwar period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Kingdom_of_Italy_1942_with_provinces.svg/250px-Kingdom_of_Italy_1942_with_provinces.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Kingdom_of_Italy_1942_with_provinces.svg)

Provinces of Italy in 1942 during World War II

In 1927, after a [royal charter](/wiki/Royal_charter "Royal charter"),[[Note 1]](#cite_note-15) a general province rearrangement took place. 17 new provinces were created: [Aosta](/wiki/Valle_d%27Aosta "Valle d'Aosta"), [Vercelli](/wiki/Province_of_Vercelli "Province of Vercelli"), [Varese](/wiki/Province_of_Varese "Province of Varese"), [Savona](/wiki/Province_of_Savona "Province of Savona"), [Bolzano](/wiki/Province_of_Bolzano "Province of Bolzano"), [Gorizia](/wiki/Province_of_Gorizia "Province of Gorizia"), [Pistoia](/wiki/Province_of_Pistoia "Province of Pistoia"), [Pescara](/wiki/Province_of_Pescara "Province of Pescara"), [Rieti](/wiki/Province_of_Rieti "Province of Rieti"), [Terni](/wiki/Province_of_Terni "Province of Terni"), [Viterbo](/wiki/Province_of_Viterbo "Province of Viterbo"), [Frosinone](/wiki/Province_of_Frosinone "Province of Frosinone"), [Brindisi](/wiki/Province_of_Brindisi "Province of Brindisi"), [Matera](/wiki/Province_of_Matera "Province of Matera"), [Ragusa](/wiki/Province_of_Ragusa "Province of Ragusa"), [Castrogiovanni](/wiki/Province_of_Enna "Province of Enna"), [Nuoro](/wiki/Province_of_Nuoro "Province of Nuoro"). In the same year, the [province of Caserta](/wiki/Province_of_Caserta "Province of Caserta") was dissolved, Girgenti was renamed [Agrigento](/wiki/Province_of_Agrigento "Province of Agrigento"), and the institution of *circondari*, sub-provincial wards created before the [unification](/wiki/Italian_Unification "Italian Unification"), was abolished.

In 1930 Spezia became [La Spezia](/wiki/Province_of_La_Spezia "Province of La Spezia"), while in 1931 Bari delle Puglie became [Bari](/wiki/Province_of_Bari "Province of Bari"). [Province of Littoria](/wiki/Province_of_Latina "Province of Latina") (Latina) was created in 1934, and the [province of Asti](/wiki/Province_of_Asti "Province of Asti") in 1935. In 1939 the province of Aquila degli Abruzzi became the [province of L'Aquila](/wiki/Province_of_L%27Aquila "Province of L'Aquila"), and in 1940 the province of Friuli was renamed the [province of Udine](/wiki/Province_of_Udine "Province of Udine").

Following the annexation of a part of [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia") in 1941, during the [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), the [province of Zara](/wiki/Province_of_Zara "Province of Zara") was enlarged and joined the [Governorate of Dalmatia](/wiki/Governorate_of_Dalmatia "Governorate of Dalmatia") (comprising the province of [Zara](/wiki/Province_of_Zara "Province of Zara"), and the new provinces of [Spalato](/wiki/Province_of_Spalato "Province of Spalato"), and [Cattaro](/wiki/Province_of_Cattaro "Province of Cattaro")), while in the occupied central part of the present-day [Slovenia](/wiki/Slovenia "Slovenia") the new [province of Ljubljana](/wiki/Province_of_Ljubljana "Province of Ljubljana") was created. This lasted only until 1945, when Yugoslavia regained the lost territories after the end of the World War II.

### After World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: After World War II")]

In 1945, after the end of the World War II, the province of Aosta changed its name to [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley") and Littoria to [Latina](/wiki/Province_of_Latina "Province of Latina"); the new province of [Caserta](/wiki/Province_of_Caserta "Province of Caserta") was recreated.

With the [Paris Peace Treaties](/wiki/Paris_Peace_Treaties,_1947 "Paris Peace Treaties, 1947"), signed on 10 February 1947, Italy lost the provinces of [Fiume](/wiki/Province_of_Fiume "Province of Fiume"), [Pola](/wiki/Province_of_Pola "Province of Pola"), and [Zara](/wiki/Province_of_Zara "Province of Zara"), and part of the provinces of [Trieste](/wiki/Province_of_Trieste "Province of Trieste") and [Gorizia](/wiki/Province_of_Gorizia "Province of Gorizia").

Moreover, the province of Trieste was occupied by [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") and [British](/wiki/United_Kingdom "United Kingdom") forces. The Italian Republic therefore had 91 provinces at its birth. The province of Ionio was renamed as [Taranto](/wiki/Province_of_Taranto "Province of Taranto") in 1951, and in 1954 the [province of Trieste](/wiki/Province_of_Trieste "Province of Trieste") was returned to Italy.

### Recent history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Recent history")]

The [province of Pordenone](/wiki/Province_of_Pordenone "Province of Pordenone") was created in 1968, the [province of Isernia](/wiki/Province_of_Isernia "Province of Isernia") in 1970, and the [province of Oristano](/wiki/Province_of_Oristano "Province of Oristano") in 1974. In a reorganization in 1992 eight provinces were created: [Verbano-Cusio-Ossola](/wiki/Province_of_Verbano-Cusio-Ossola "Province of Verbano-Cusio-Ossola"), [Biella](/wiki/Province_of_Biella "Province of Biella"), [Lecco](/wiki/Province_of_Lecco "Province of Lecco"), [Lodi](/wiki/Province_of_Lodi "Province of Lodi"), [Rimini](/wiki/Province_of_Rimini "Province of Rimini"), [Prato](/wiki/Province_of_Prato "Province of Prato"), [Crotone](/wiki/Province_of_Crotone "Province of Crotone"), and [Vibo Valentia](/wiki/Province_of_Vibo_Valentia "Province of Vibo Valentia"), while Forlì was renamed as [Forlì-Cesena](/wiki/Provincia_di_Forl%C3%AC-Cesena "Provincia di Forlì-Cesena").

Four new provinces were created in Sardinia in 2001, with effect from 2005: [Olbia-Tempio](/wiki/Province_of_Olbia-Tempio "Province of Olbia-Tempio"), [Ogliastra](/wiki/Province_of_Ogliastra "Province of Ogliastra"), [Medio Campidano](/wiki/Province_of_Medio_Campidano "Province of Medio Campidano"), and [Carbonia-Iglesias](/wiki/Province_of_Carbonia-Iglesias "Province of Carbonia-Iglesias"). In 2004 three further provinces were created: [Monza and Brianza](/wiki/Province_of_Monza_e_Brianza "Province of Monza e Brianza"), [Fermo](/wiki/Province_of_Fermo "Province of Fermo"), and [Barletta-Andria-Trani](/wiki/Province_of_Barletta-Andria-Trani "Province of Barletta-Andria-Trani"), making a total of 110 provinces.

In May 2012, a referendum abolished the eight provinces of Sardinia, and this suppression was to take effect on 1 March 2013. On 6 July 2012, new plans were published to reduce the number of provinces by around half.[[15]](#cite_note-16) In January 2014 the Sardinian Regional Administrative Court declared "unconstitutional" the abolition of the Sardinian provinces.[[16]](#cite_note-17)

In 2014 the Delrio Law[[17]](#cite_note-18) transformed the provinces of Italy in a reduced number of broader administrative entities.[[18]](#cite_note-provinceditalia.it-19)

In 2014 the [Friuli-Venezia Giulia](/wiki/Friuli-Venezia_Giulia "Friuli-Venezia Giulia") of [Debora Serracchiani](/wiki/Debora_Serracchiani "Debora Serracchiani") was the first Italian region to pass a law for abolishing its provinces, while implementing the national reform in the local administrative level.[[18]](#cite_note-provinceditalia.it-19) The Friuli region has multiplied four provinces in 18 unions of the Italian administrative unit called *[comune](/wiki/Comune "Comune")*.[[19]](#cite_note-20) After rejection of the [2016 Italian constitutional referendum](/wiki/2016_Italian_constitutional_referendum#CNEL_and_provinces "2016 Italian constitutional referendum"), the provinces of Italy were still kept alive under provisions of the Delrio Constitutional Law to be merged in a smaller number of union of provinces.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

## Former provinces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Former provinces")]

### Historical abolished provinces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Historical abolished provinces")]

* [Province of Aosta](/wiki/Province_of_Aosta "Province of Aosta") (Italian: *provincia di Aosta*) (1927–1945). Became the Autonomous Region of [Aosta Valley](/wiki/Aosta_Valley "Aosta Valley") in 1948.
* Province of Terra di Lavoro (Italian: *provincia di Terra di Lavoro*) (1861–1927). It was divided into the current provinces of [Frosinone](/wiki/Province_of_Frosinone "Province of Frosinone"), [Latina](/wiki/Province_of_Latina "Province of Latina"), and [Caserta](/wiki/Province_of_Caserta "Province of Caserta").

### Provinces of Istria, Kvarner, and Dalmatia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Provinces of Istria, Kvarner, and Dalmatia")]

* [Province of Zara](/wiki/Province_of_Zara "Province of Zara") (Italian: *provincia di Zara*) (1923–1947). Created after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") in [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia"). Originally a small territory, it was greatly enlarged in 1941 during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). It was a part of the [Governorship of Dalmatia](/wiki/Governorship_of_Dalmatia "Governorship of Dalmatia"). It remained nominally a part of the [Italian Social Republic](/wiki/Italian_Social_Republic "Italian Social Republic") after the [Italian capitulation](/wiki/Italian_capitulation "Italian capitulation").
* [Province of Pola](/wiki/Province_of_Pola "Province of Pola") (Italian: *provincia di Pola*) (1923–1947). Created after World War I in [Istria](/wiki/Istria "Istria"). It was occupied by [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") in September 1943 and it was administered as a part of the German [Operational Zone of the Adriatic Littoral](/wiki/Operational_Zone_of_the_Adriatic_Littoral "Operational Zone of the Adriatic Littoral").
* [Province of Fiume](/wiki/Province_of_Fiume "Province of Fiume") (Italian: *provincia di Fiume*) (1924–1947). Created after World War I in [Kvarner](/wiki/Kvarner "Kvarner"). Enlarged during World War II. It was occupied by Germany in September 1943 and it was administered as a part of the German Operational Zone of the Adriatic Littoral.

### Provinces established during World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Provinces established during World War II")]

Further information: [Invasion of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia")

* [Province of Ljubljana](/wiki/Province_of_Ljubljana "Province of Ljubljana") (Italian: *provincia di Lubiana*) (1941–1943). Created during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"). It was occupied by Germany in September 1943 and it was administered as a part of the German [Operation Zone of the Adriatic Littoral](/wiki/Operation_Zone_of_the_Adriatic_Littoral "Operation Zone of the Adriatic Littoral").
* [Province of Spalato](/wiki/Province_of_Spalato "Province of Spalato") (Italian: *provincia di Spalato*) (1941–1943). Created during World War II. It was a part of the [Governorship of Dalmatia](/wiki/Governorship_of_Dalmatia "Governorship of Dalmatia"). It was occupied by Germany in September 1943 and later annexed by the [Independent State of Croatia](/wiki/Independent_State_of_Croatia "Independent State of Croatia").
* [Province of Cattaro](/wiki/Province_of_Cattaro "Province of Cattaro") (Italian: *provincia di Cattaro*) (1941–1943). Created during World War II. It was a part of the Governorship of Dalmatia. It was occupied by Germany in September 1943 and partially annexed by the Independent State of Croatia.

Further information: [Italian occupation of France during World War II](/wiki/Italian_occupation_of_France_during_World_War_II "Italian occupation of France during World War II"), [Invasion of Yugoslavia](/wiki/Invasion_of_Yugoslavia "Invasion of Yugoslavia"), [Greco-Italian War](/wiki/Greco-Italian_War "Greco-Italian War"), and [German invasion of Greece](/wiki/German_invasion_of_Greece "German invasion of Greece")

* Province of the [Western Alps](/wiki/Western_Alps "Western Alps") (Italian: *provincia delle Alpi Occidentali*). Planned [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") province to be created of the Italian-annexed French territories of the [Alpes Maritimes](/wiki/Alpes_Maritimes "Alpes Maritimes") (including the [Principality of Monaco](/wiki/Principality_of_Monaco "Principality of Monaco")) and parts of [Alpes-de-Haute-Provence](/wiki/Alpes-de-Haute-Provence "Alpes-de-Haute-Provence"), [Hautes-Alpes](/wiki/Hautes-Alpes "Hautes-Alpes"), and [Savoie](/wiki/Savoie "Savoie").[[21]](#cite_note-rodogno-22) The town of [Briançon](/wiki/Brian%C3%A7on "Briançon") (Italian: *Brianzone*) was to act as the provincial capital.[[21]](#cite_note-rodogno-22)
* Province of [Corsica](/wiki/Corsica "Corsica") (Italian: *provincia della Corsica*). Planned to be created after World War II [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") victory, with [Petru Giovacchini](/wiki/Petru_Giovacchini "Petru Giovacchini") as possible "governor".
* Province of [Ragusa](/wiki/Dubrovnik "Dubrovnik") in [Dalmatia](/wiki/Dalmatia "Dalmatia") (Italian: *provincia di Ragusa di Dalmazia*). Planned World War II province to be created of the Italian-annexed Dalmatian territories that were areas of the ancient [Republic of Ragusa](/wiki/Republic_of_Ragusa "Republic of Ragusa").
* Provinces in islands of Greece: *provincia delle Ionie*; *provincia delle Cicladi*; *provincia di Samo*. Planned World War II provinces to be created of the Italian-annexed islands of [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") ([Ionian Islands](/wiki/Ionian_Islands "Ionian Islands"), [Cyclades](/wiki/Cyclades "Cyclades"), and [Samos](/wiki/Samos "Samos")).

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Provinces_of_Italy&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Controversies")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b9/ITALIAN_PROVINCES.png/250px-ITALIAN_PROVINCES.png)](/wiki/File:ITALIAN_PROVINCES.png)

Provinces as proposed by the [Monti Cabinet](/wiki/Monti_Cabinet "Monti Cabinet") in 2012

Provinces are often deemed useless by their critics, and many proposals were made in the 2010s to eliminate them.[[22]](#cite_note-contro1-23)[[23]](#cite_note-contro2-24)[[24]](#cite_note-contro3-25) The difficulty of changing the [Constitution of Italy](/wiki/Constitution_of_Italy "Constitution of Italy") and the opposition of groups of politicians and citizens halted any proposal of reform.[[25]](#cite_note-contro4-26)[[26]](#cite_note-FFstoria-27)

In 2013, during his speech to the Chamber of Deputies, [Enrico Letta](/wiki/Enrico_Letta "Enrico Letta"), the newly appointed [Prime Minister of Italy](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Italy "Prime Minister of Italy"), announced that a revision of the second part of the constitution was needed, in order to change the bicameral parliamentary system and to abolish the provinces. The proposal, presented during the [Renzi government](/wiki/Renzi_government "Renzi government"), was rejected in the [2016 Italian constitutional referendum](/wiki/2016_Italian_constitutional_referendum "2016 Italian constitutional referendum") held on 4 December.
